---
title: "Helen Webley-Brown"
image: "website-pic.jpg"
output:
  postcards::trestles
---
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```
I am a S.M. student in Political Science at MIT with specializations in American Politics and Quantitative Methods. I am also an affiliate of the [*Center for American Political Studies*](https://caps.gov.harvard.edu/){style="color:#005354"} at Harvard University. In 2022, I graduated from Washington University in St. Louis with a A.B. in Political Science.

Although my research focuses on the US, I grew up in London, England. I moved across the pond for college via the US-UK Fulbright Commission's wonderful [*Sutton Trust US Programme*](https://us.suttontrust.com/){style="color:#005354"}. My personal background has greatly shaped my commitment to inclusive pedagogy and widening access to higher education.

Currently, my research agenda fits into two broad buckets:

1.  The socio-political effects of interacting with local institutions of social control, focusing on [police departments]{style="color:teal"} and [public housing authorities]{style="color:teal"}.
2.  How [emerging technologies]{style="color:teal"} change the way people experience and perceive the state.

At the intersection of this, I am interested in the participatory implications of tools like ShotSpotter in policing, and Landlord Tech in subsidized housing. Parallel to studies of demobilization, I also ask: under what conditions can marginalized communities carve out democratic practices within systems that control, surveil, or punish them? I have two early-stage projects that grapple with this question. One project uses topic modelling and Random Forest to analyze Community Control Over Police Surveillance (CCOPS) adoption, while the other leverages a novel dataset to examine resident council emergence in public housing developments.
